notes.anchor: how great a weight your authority has always carried with me
notes.note: Marcellus's own reply, written from Mytilene in October 46 BC, answering Cicero's run of letters (Fam. 4.7-4.10). He concedes that Cicero's auctoritas finally overcame his reluctance to accept Caesar's pardon and return from exile.

notes.anchor: When my brother Gaius Marcellus, who loves me most warmly
notes.note: C. Claudius Marcellus, consul 50 BC (his cousin, called frater), had pleaded for the recall both by counsel and by his famous tearful supplication of Caesar in the Senate; yet Marcellus says even that did not persuade him until Cicero's letters tipped the balance.

notes.anchor: until your letter brought it about that the counsel I followed was, before all else, yours and his
notes.note: "How the affair was managed" alludes to the Senate session of September 46 BC, when Caesar, moved by the appeals of C. Marcellus and the whole house, granted his pardon — the moment that prompted Cicero's speech of thanks, the Pro Marcello.

notes.anchor: out of the very small number of friends, kinsmen, and connections who truly favoured my restoration
notes.note: Marcellus's pointed remark on the smallness of his loyal circle reflects the bitterness of exile: he singles out Cicero as the most eager and warm-hearted of the few who genuinely worked for his return (salus, here "restoration").
